commit 7b2e91cd955422cfffd9b209f729b9f6165a2219
Author: Mikhail Gavrilov <mikhail.v.gavrilov@gmail.com>

mm/slab: align kmalloc to cacheline when DMA API debugging is active

This commit changes ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN to L1_CACHE_BYTES when
CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG is enabled to avoid false positive DMA overlapping
warnings.

Link: https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=215740

> diff --git a/include/linux/slab.h b/include/linux/slab.h
> index 15a60b501b95..f044956e17c1 100644
> --- a/include/linux/slab.h
> +++ b/include/linux/slab.h
> @@ -536,6 +536,19 @@ static inline bool kmem_dump_obj(void *object) { return false; }
>  #endif
>  #endif
>  
> +/*
> + * Align memory allocations to cache lines if DMA API debugging is active
> + * to avoid false positive DMA overlapping error messages.
> + */
> +#ifdef CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG
> +#ifndef ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN
> +#define ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN  L1_CACHE_BYTES
> +#elif ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN < L1_CACHE_BYTES
> +#undef ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN
> +#define ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN  L1_CACHE_BYTES

Can redefining ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN in a generic header cause silent ABI
mismatches and ODR violations?

Because ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN is natively defined in architecture headers
like asm/cache.h, C files that do not include linux/slab.h (or include
other headers before it) will see the original value. Files including
linux/slab.h first will see the new value.

If structures use ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN or derived macros like
CRYPTO_MINALIGN in linux/crypto.h for array sizing or alignment, their
sizes and layouts will differ across object files.

Does inflating the alignment to silence a debug warning mask the actual bugs
the debug option is meant to detect?

CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG is designed to detect unsafe behavior, such as
overlapping cachelines in DMA mappings. By artificially inflating
ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN to L1_CACHE_BYTES when this debug option is enabled,
the allocator prevents small allocations from sharing a cacheline.

This hides the overlap during debug builds so the API will not warn.
However, in production builds where the debug option is disabled, the
allocations will share a cacheline, which could lead to real data corruption.

Does this change defeat the testing of DMA bounce buffers for unaligned
kmallocs?

Architectures like arm64 decouple kmalloc alignment from DMA alignment
and handle unaligned DMA mappings using bounce buffers, for example
when using CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC.

By forcing all kmalloc allocations to be cacheline-aligned when
CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG is enabled, the unaligned kmalloc bounce-buffer code
paths will never be exercised, removing test coverage for the exact DMA
machinery the debug option is meant to validate.

Will this cause unnecessary system-wide memory bloat for small allocations?

Changing ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN to L1_CACHE_BYTES system-wide forces the
smallest kmalloc cache to that size. Every 8-byte, 16-byte, or 32-byte
allocation will consume a full cacheline, dramatically increasing the
memory footprint and cache pressure across the entire system when the
debug option is enabled.

> +#endif
> +#endif
> +
>  #ifndef ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN
>  #define ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN __alignof__(unsigned long long)
>  #elif ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN > 8

Target Commit:
commit 7b2e91cd955422cfffd9b209f729b9f6165a2219
Author: Mikhail Gavrilov <mikhail.v.gavrilov@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Mar 27 05:58:46 2026 +0000

    mm/slab: align kmalloc to cacheline when DMA API debugging is active
    
    When CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG is enabled, the DMA debug infrastructure
    tracks active mappings per cacheline and warns if two different DMA
    mappings share the same cacheline ("cacheline tracking EEXIST,
    overlapping mappings aren't supported").
    
    On x86_64, ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN defaults to 8, so small kmalloc
    allocations (e.g. the 8-byte hub->buffer and hub->status in the USB
    hub driver) frequently land in the same 64-byte cacheline.  When both
    are DMA-mapped, this triggers a false positive warning.
    
    This has been reported repeatedly since v5.14 (when the EEXIST check
    was added) across various USB host controllers and devices including
    xhci_hcd with USB hubs, USB audio devices, and USB ethernet adapters.
    
    Raise ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN to L1_CACHE_BYTES when CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG
    is enabled, ensuring each kmalloc allocation occupies its own cacheline
    and eliminating the false positive.
    
    Verified with a kernel module reproducer that performs two kmalloc(8)
    allocations back-to-back and DMA-maps both:
    
      Before: allocations share a cacheline, EEXIST fires within ~50 pairs
      After:  64 pairs allocated, all in separate cachelines, no warning
    
    Fixes: 2b4bbc6231d7 ("dma-debug: report -EEXIST errors in add_dma_entry")
    Link: https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=215740
    Suggested-by: Alan Stern <stern@rowland.harvard.edu>
    Suggested-by: Guenter Roeck <linux@roeck-us.net>
    Tested-by: Jeff Kirsher <Jeff.kirsher@gmail.com>
    Tested-by: Mikhail Gavrilov <mikhail.v.gavrilov@gmail.com>
    Signed-off-by: Mikhail Gavrilov <mikhail.v.gavrilov@gmail.com>

diff --git a/include/linux/slab.h b/include/linux/slab.h
index 15a60b501b95..f044956e17c1 100644
--- a/include/linux/slab.h
+++ b/include/linux/slab.h
@@ -536,6 +536,19 @@ static inline bool kmem_dump_obj(void *object) { return false; }
 #endif
 #endif
 
+/*
+ * Align memory allocations to cache lines if DMA API debugging is active
+ * to avoid false positive DMA overlapping error messages.
+ */
+#ifdef CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG
+#ifndef ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN
+#define ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN  L1_CACHE_BYTES
+#elif ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN < L1_CACHE_BYTES
+#undef ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN
+#define ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN  L1_CACHE_BYTES
+#endif
+#endif
+
 #ifndef ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN
 #define ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN __alignof__(unsigned long long)
 #elif ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN > 8

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Impact of `CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG` on DMA Alignment and Cache Coherency**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `dma_kmalloc_needs_bounce` check. If we increase `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` to `L1_CACHE_BYTES`, small allocations get rounded up and `dma_debug` might stop warning. But then I'm thinking about the implications. On ARM64, `dma_get_cache_alignment()` is 128, so bumping `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` to 64 could still trigger a bounce if the allocation size causes it.

The thing is, `dma_debug` is hardcoded to use `L1_CACHE_BYTES` for cacheline granularity. It only checks overlaps at that level. Thus it assumes that the cacheline size equals `L1_CACHE_BYTES`. If we adjust `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` to match this, then `kmalloc(8)` and `kmalloc(8)` might never share the same cacheline, silencing the warning.

But here's the core issue: is it right to change `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` just because `CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG` is enabled? `CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG` is meant to *debug* drivers, not fundamentally alter behavior. On x86, which is coherent, sharing a cacheline in this context is perfectly safe. The debug infrastructure is to find code that *might* be used on non-coherent architectures. So, modifying `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` just hides a legitimate warning. The driver isn't doing anything wrong.

Instead of changing the alignment, the more logical approach would be to make `dma_debug` smarter. It should *not* warn for `kmalloc` allocations on coherent architectures like x86, but it needs to check for code that *might* fail on a non-coherent system. However, there isn't a check for coherent devices in `active_cacheline_insert`. That's because `dma_debug`'s design aims to find issues that could crop up in different architectures.

Here's the problem: The current logic of `dma_debug` running on x86, doesn't know that if this same code was running on a non-coherent architecture, alignment would have caused it to be handled differently. It incorrectly assumes x86's memory layout is the same as an ARM layout without bounce buffers, which is false.

The patch's proposed solution is to force `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` to `L1_CACHE_BYTES` when `DMA_API_DEBUG` is on. This increases memory usage by rounding up small allocations. This could be acceptable since the debug mode itself already incurs memory overhead.

So the main thing to consider is the impact: it dramatically increases `kmalloc` size for many small allocations. But is it safe? We could `#undef` and redefine the value, but does it affect the entire kernel? It seems like it would; `include/linux/slab.h` is included almost everywhere. The real risk is files that include `<asm/cache.h>` but *not* `<linux/slab.h>`.

In conclusion, this approach seems to be a valid, though somewhat crude, attempt to silence the warning.

    "thought": "**Alignment Shenanigans: A Preprocessor Nightmare**

Okay, so I've been digging into this code, specifically around memory alignment and the `kmalloc` allocator, and I've stumbled upon a real preprocessor headache. My initial investigation of `rust/kernel/alloc/allocator.rs` led me down a rabbit hole involving `include/linux/crypto.h` and the macro `CRYPTO_MINALIGN`. The key issue is with the definition and redefinition of `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN`. It looks like if a file includes `<linux/crypto.h>` *before* `<linux/slab.h>`, it picks up the original value of `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN`.

Here's the problem: C macros are evaluated at the point of expansion, which means the value of `CRYPTO_MINALIGN` (defined as `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` in `crypto.h`) is determined by what `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` *currently* is. If `<linux/slab.h>` is included *after* `crypto.h`, that includes a redefinition of `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN`, the value of `CRYPTO_MINALIGN` will change.

If `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` starts at 8, and is later redefined to 64 (in `<linux/slab.h>`), that creates an inconsistent definition of the macro depending on the translation unit's include order. This is a massive concern! It’s like a time bomb waiting to explode. Imagine files like `drivers/usb/misc/usbtest.c` or `drivers/crypto/caam/caamalg.c` using the *old* value, while `mm/slab_common.c` is using the *new* value!

Now, the alignment bound calculations with `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` might technically succeed if it's used as a mask, because 64 is a multiple of 8, so 8-byte alignment logic will technically succeed. But using `BUILD_BUG_ON` could fail or succeed differently depending on the inclusion order! This could lead to ODR violations!

The suggested fix proposes modifying `KMALLOC_MIN_SIZE` based on `CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG`, which sounds better than the original idea. The current implementation in `include/linux/slab.h` is this:

```c
#ifndef ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN
#define ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN __alignof__(unsigned long long)
#elif ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN > 8
#define KMALLOC_MIN_SIZE ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN
#define KMALLOC_SHIFT_LOW ilog2(KMALLOC_MIN_SIZE)
#endif
```

I *think* we should modify it to change `KMALLOC_MIN_SIZE` only in the event of `CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG`. However, the current patch attempts to do this:

```c
#ifdef CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG
#ifndef ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN
#define ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN  L1_CACHE_BYTES
#elif ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN < L1_CACHE_BYTES
#undef ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN
#define ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN  L1_CACHE_BYTES
#endif
#endif
```

That's a **bad** idea.  It redefines `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` ONLY for files that include `<linux/slab.h>`.  This means files that use `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` but don't include `<linux/slab.h>` see the old definition and cause inconsistent behavior. It’s a textbook example of a C preprocessor anti-pattern.  We need to alter the value of the macro in the architecture configuration or a single well-defined architecture header.

Looking at `mm/slab_common.c`, it's clear the alignment of *all* caches (not just `kmalloc`) is calculated using `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN`.  The patch would make all `kmalloc` allocations to `L1_CACHE_BYTES`, which is fine, but it would also change the alignment of other objects that don't need that.

Ultimately, the attempt to redefine `ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN` within `slab.h` is deeply flawed.  It causes subtle but critical inconsistencies that could lead to memory corruption or, at the very least, unpredictable behavior. Using the preprocessor to redefine an architecture-level macro in this way is a disaster waiting to happen.
